title: akaitools: A Python package for parsing and analyzing AkaiKKR electronic structure calculations
summary: The Korringa-Kohn-Rostoker (KKR) Green's function method is a first-principles electronic structure approach well suited to substitutionally disordered alloys through the Coherent Potential Approximation (CPA). AkaiKKR is a widely used implementation, known for efficient treatment of metallic systems and their magnetic properties. Its output, however, is unstructured plain text with no programmatic interface, leaving data extraction entirely to the user and making systematic or high-throughput studies impractical. akaitools is a Python package that parses AkaiKKR output files into structured, type-annotated Python objects. The package covers three output types: self-consistent field (SCF) results, which capture convergence history and per-atom electronic and magnetic properties; spin-resolved, orbital projected density of states for each CPA component; and Bloch spectral functions on a user-defined k-point path. Results come back as immutable dataclasses backed by NumPy arrays. Energy quantities are available in both Rydbergs and electronvolts, and results can be exported to Pandas DataFrames. A built-in plotting module produces Matplotlib figures for DOS curves and SCF convergence. A command-line interface provides file summaries and JSON export without any Python scripting. The package also includes a programmatic input file generator, so full calculation pipelines from input preparation to output analysis can be run in Python.

AI.tldr: akaitools is a Python package that parses unstructured plain text output from the AkaiKKR electronic structure code into structured, type-annotated Python objects, enabling systematic and high-throughput studies.
AI.motivation: AkaiKKR is a widely used implementation of the KKR Green's function method for substitutionally disordered alloys, but its output is unstructured plain text with no programmatic interface, making data extraction manual and high-throughput studies impractical.
AI.method: The authors developed a Python package called akaitools that parses AkaiKKR output files into immutable dataclasses backed by NumPy arrays. The package covers three output types: SCF results (convergence history and per-atom properties), spin-resolved/orbital projected density of states for each CPA component, and Bloch spectral functions on user-defined k-point paths. Energy quantities are available in both Rydbergs and electronvolts, with export capability to Pandas DataFrames. A built-in plotting module produces Matplotlib figures, and a CLI provides file summaries and JSON export. The package also includes a programmatic input file generator.
AI.result: akaitools enables full calculation pipelines from input preparation to output analysis to be run in Python, with data available as structured objects rather than plain text.
AI.conclusion: akaitools addresses the usability gap in AkaiKKR by providing a programmatic interface, enabling systematic and high-throughput first-principles studies of metallic systems and their magnetic properties.

title: Magnetotransport Measurements on Isolated Polycrystalline Grains of Type-II Silicon Clathrate
summary: The first low-temperature electronic transport characterization of individual polycrystalline grains of type-II silicon clathrate (Na$_x$Si$_{136}$, $x \ll 1$), isolated using microfabrication techniques, is reported. Structural characterization via Raman spectroscopy confirms that the isolated grains are largely devoid of amorphous silicon (a-Si). Temperature-dependent resistivity reveals multiple conduction regimes, including thermally activated freeze-out behavior and a transition to low-activation-energy transport at cryogenic temperatures, consistent with hopping conduction mechanisms. Hall measurements from 290 K to 3.5 K yield carrier concentration and mobility trends that correlate with the extracted activation energies, verifying $n$-type conduction. Additionally, gate-dependent conductivity measurements demonstrate electrostatic tunability at room temperature. Collectively, these results establish the magnetotransport parameters of single, isolated grains of type-II silicon clathrate and demonstrate the potential of this material for future quantum and optoelectronic devices.

AI.tldr: First low-temperature electronic transport study of individual type-II silicon clathrate (Na_xSi_136) grains using microfabrication techniques, revealing multiple conduction regimes and n-type behavior with electrostatic tunability.
AI.motivation: To establish the fundamental magnetotransport properties of single, isolated grains of type-II silicon clathrate for potential quantum and optoelectronic applications, as previous studies were limited to bulk or polycrystalline samples.
AI.method: Microfabrication techniques to isolate individual polycrystalline grains; Raman spectroscopy for structural characterization; temperature-dependent resistivity measurements (290 K to 3.5 K); Hall effect measurements; gate-dependent conductivity measurements.
AI.result: Multiple conduction regimes observed: thermally activated freeze-out behavior at higher temperatures transitioning to low-activation-energy hopping conduction at cryogenic temperatures. Hall measurements confirm n-type conduction with carrier concentration and mobility trends correlating with activation energies. Electrostatic tunability demonstrated at room temperature.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes the essential magnetotransport parameters of single, isolated type-II silicon clathrate grains and demonstrates their potential for future quantum and optoelectronic devices.

title: Hydration-controlled twist forms a moiré glass in charge-frustrated layered silicates
comment: 25 pages, 4 main figures, 8 supplementary figures, 1 supplementary table
summary: Twisting layered materials produces moiré superlattices, but prescribed twist angles are usually obtained by demanding assembly procedures. Here we show that montmorillonite, an abundant swelling clay, forms tunable moiré superlattices naturally. Focal-series high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, geometric phase analysis, and molecular dynamics simulation reveal that its apparent rotational disorder is biased toward low-angle misorientations inherited from discrete hydration states. Multilayer stacks preferentially adopt twists near 1-2°, 4°, and 10°, producing long-wavelength moirés without long-range rotational order. We define this kinetically trapped state as a moiré glass, distinct from featureless turbostratic stacking. Simulations indicate that lattice-charge disorder stabilizes the angular preferences, whereas charge ordering promotes random stacking. Hydration screens interlayer interactions and lubricates twist, while dehydration arrests the resulting configurations in discrete steps. These results establish dynamic hydration as a macroscopic handle for programming twist in layered matter.

title: Chemical Vapor Deposition of Ni-doped Iron Germanium Telluride Nanosheets
summary: Iron germanium telluride (FGT; FemGenTe2) compounds have attracted significant interest due to their layered van der Waals structure, relatively high Curie temperature, and tunable magnetic properties. Chemical vapor deposition (CVD) is a particularly promising synthesis route owing to its simplicity, low cost, potential for scalability, and widespread adoption in the semiconductor industry, yet it has not been used previously to synthesize FGT with dopants. Here, we report CVD synthesis of both undoped and Ni-doped FGT nanosheets on SiO2/Si substrates. By adjusting precursor molar ratios, we synthesized Ni-doped FGT with multiple Fe concentrations and a 4% Ni-to-Fe ratio. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy depth profiling further demonstrates that Ni is present in the bulk of the crystals. This straightforward, low-cost, and CMOS-compatible approach demonstrates a route to Ni-doped FGT nanosheets, establishing a foundation for future characterization of Ni-doped FGT and its potential integration into spintronic devices.

AI.tldr: This paper reports the first chemical vapor deposition (CVD) synthesis of Ni-doped iron germanium telluride (FGT) nanosheets. By adjusting precursor molar ratios, the researchers achieved Ni-doped FGT with a 4% Ni-to-Fe ratio, where Ni is present in the bulk of the crystals as confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy depth profiling.
AI.motivation: FGT compounds are promising for spintronic applications due to their layered van der Waals structure, relatively high Curie temperature, and tunable magnetic properties. However, CVD synthesis had not been previously used to dope FGT with transition metals like Ni, despite CVD's advantages of simplicity, low cost, scalability, and CMOS compatibility.
AI.method: The researchers used chemical vapor deposition (CVD) to synthesize both undoped and Ni-doped FGT nanosheets on SiO2/Si substrates. They adjusted precursor molar ratios to achieve multiple Fe concentrations and a 4% Ni-to-Fe ratio. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy depth profiling was used to analyze the composition and distribution of Ni in the crystals.
AI.result: Successfully synthesized Ni-doped FGT nanosheets via CVD with multiple Fe concentrations. A 4% Ni-to-Fe ratio was achieved. XPS depth profiling confirmed that Ni is present in the bulk of the crystals, not just on the surface.
AI.conclusion: This straightforward, low-cost, and CMOS-compatible CVD approach demonstrates a viable route to Ni-doped FGT nanosheets, establishing a foundation for future characterization of Ni-doped FGT and its potential integration into spintronic devices.

title: Magnetic field induced transition from nodeless to nodal superconductivity in $β$-PdBi$_{2}$
summary: Recent tunneling measurements on $β$-PdBi$_2$ reported a magnetic field induced phase transition from a fully gapped to a nodal superconducting state. We develop a microscopic theory of superconductivity in this multi-band material, taking into account spin-orbit coupling and Zeeman splitting. We show that there are two attractive pairing channels in this system: an s-wave and a p-wave. At zero magnetic field, s-wave superconductivity wins. At a finite field, this channel becomes less favorable because Fermi surfaces split, and the gap symmetry changes to p-wave. We show that at a higher field, the excitation spectrum of a p-wave multi-band superconductor becomes gapless, with nodal points located in between the split Fermi surfaces. We argue that this behavior accounts for the experimentally observed field-induced nodal superconductivity in $β$-PdBi$_2$.

AI.tldr: A microscopic theory explains the magnetic field-induced phase transition from s-wave to p-wave superconductivity in β-PdBi2, where the p-wave state exhibits gapless excitations with nodal points, accounting for experimental observations of field-induced nodal superconductivity.
AI.motivation: To explain recent tunneling measurements showing a magnetic field-induced transition from fully gapped to nodal superconducting state in β-PdBi2, which was not previously understood theoretically.
AI.method: Developed a microscopic theory of superconductivity in this multi-band material incorporating spin-orbit coupling and Zeeman splitting to study the competition between s-wave and p-wave pairing channels.
AI.result: Found two attractive pairing channels: s-wave dominates at zero field, but becomes less favorable at finite magnetic field due to Fermi surface splitting, leading to p-wave symmetry. The p-wave state exhibits gapless excitations with nodal points located between split Fermi surfaces at higher fields.
AI.conclusion: The theoretical framework successfully explains the experimentally observed field-induced nodal superconductivity in β-PdBi2, demonstrating that magnetic fields can drive phase transitions between different gap symmetries in multi-band superconductors.

title: Tunable Chaos in the Finite Mean SYK Model
summary: The complex Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model, featuring fermions with all-to-all interactions, serves as a dual paradigm for understanding non-Fermi liquid behavior and the holographic nature of charged black holes. Two defining characteristics of the standard SYK model are its maximal chaos (Lyapunov exponent $λ_{\mathrm{L}}=2πT$ at temperature $T$), and its finite zero-temperature residual entropy. While previous studies have largely focused on couplings drawn from a zero-mean Gaussian distribution, we investigate a generalized model with a finite mean-to-standard-deviation ratio, $g\equiv J_{0}/δJ$ of the coupling distribution in order to get deeper insight into the evolution of chaos. We find that increasing $g$ yields the following effects: (i) The system remains a fast scrambler with $λ_{\mathrm{L}}=A~T$, but with a suppressed coefficient $A<2π$. (ii) In the limit $g\to \infty$, out-of-time-ordered correlators (OTOCs) no longer exhibit exponential growth with $λ_{\mathrm{L}}\simeq 0$. (iii) The spectral correlations indicative of late-time chaos maintain Wigner-Dyson level spacing statistics for all values of $g$. (iv) The system preserves a finite residual entropy, albeit with reduced magnitude, for all $g$ values. We conclude that in this generalized SYK model, there is a chaotic to non-chaotic crossover. Moreover different measures of chaos decouple, demonstrating that the presence of finite residual entropy does not strictly imply maximal chaos.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates a generalized Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model with coupling distributions having a finite mean-to-standard-deviation ratio g, extending beyond the standard zero-mean Gaussian case. The study explores how varying g affects chaos indicators including the Lyapunov exponent, out-of-time-ordered correlators (OTOCs), spectral statistics, and residual entropy.
AI.motivation: The standard SYK model exhibits maximal chaos (λ_L = 2πT) and finite residual entropy, serving as a dual paradigm for non-Fermi liquids and holographic black holes. Previous studies focused on zero-mean Gaussian couplings, but the relationship between residual entropy and maximal chaos remains unclear. This work aims to understand how chaos evolves as the coupling distribution shifts from zero mean to finite mean.
AI.method: The authors studied the complex SYK model with all-to-all interactions, introducing a finite mean-to-standard-deviation ratio g = J_0/δJ in the coupling distribution. They analyzed various chaos indicators: Lyapunov exponent from OTOC exponential growth, spectral correlations via level spacing statistics (Wigner-Dyson), and zero-temperature residual entropy across different g values.
AI.result: Increasing g yields: (i) suppressed Lyapunov coefficient A < 2π while maintaining fast scrambler behavior; (ii) complete loss of exponential growth (λ_L ≃ 0) in the g→∞ limit; (iii) preserved Wigner-Dyson spectral statistics for all g; (iv) reduced but finite residual entropy for all g. A chaotic to non-chaotic crossover occurs as g increases, with different chaos measures decoupling from each other.
AI.conclusion: The generalized SYK model exhibits a continuous chaotic to non-chaotic crossover as the coupling mean increases. Critically, finite residual entropy does not strictly imply maximal chaos—the two traditional SYK characteristics can decouple. This challenges the assumed equivalence between SYK-like behavior and maximal chaos, with implications for understanding holographic duality and non-Fermi liquid physics.

title: Strain induced magnetic phase transition and anomalous transport phenomena in RuO$_2$ and MnF$_2$
summary: Collinear antiferromagnets with broken time-reversal symmetry have emerged as a fertile platform for spintronics. Using a general tight-binding model and first-principles calculations, we show that strain engineering provides a simple route to control magnetic phase transition and activate transverse responses in representative altermagnets RuO$_2$ and MnF$_2$. For pristine RuO$_2$ and MnF$_2$ with Néel vector $\mathbf{n}\parallel$ [001], symmetry constrains the off-diagonal elements of the Hall conductivity tensor to vanish, thereby forbidding anomalous transport and magneto-optical responses. Shear strain applied along the $ac$ direction preserves the spin symmetry relating the two spin-opposite magnetic sublattices and therefore maintains the altermagnetic phase. By contrast, shear strain applied along the $ab$ direction breaks this spin symmetry and drives a transition from an altermagnetic phase to a partially compensated ferrimagnetic phase in metallic RuO$_2$ and to a fully compensated ferrimagnetic phase in semiconducting MnF$_2$. In addition, the lowered symmetry enables finite anomalous Hall, anomalous Nernst, and anomalous thermal Hall conductivities, as well as magneto-optical rotation angles, which are prohibited in the pristine systems. These responses exhibit a clear strain dependence and become progressively stronger as the strain amplitude increases. Our results establish strain engineering as an effective route to manipulate magnetic phases and functional responses in unconventional antiferromagnets, thereby expanding opportunities for antiferromagnetic spintronics and magneto-optical applications.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates strain engineering as a method to control magnetic phase transitions and activate transverse transport and magneto-optical responses in altermagnets RuO₂ and MnF₂, using tight-binding models and first-principles calculations.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to overcome symmetry constraints in pristine altermagnets that forbid anomalous transport and magneto-optical responses, and to expand applications for antiferromagnetic spintronics.
AI.method: The study employs a general tight-binding model combined with first-principles calculations to investigate the effects of shear strain on magnetic properties and transport responses.
AI.result: Shear strain along the ab direction breaks spin symmetry and drives transitions from altermagnetic to ferrimagnetic phases in both RuO₂ (metallic) and MnF₂ (semiconducting), while enabling finite anomalous Hall, Nernst, and thermal Hall conductivities, as well as magneto-optical rotation angles.
AI.conclusion: Strain engineering is an effective route to manipulate magnetic phases and functional responses in unconventional antiferromagnets, opening new opportunities for antiferromagnetic spintronics and magneto-optical applications.

title: Time-resolved synchronization analysis of stacked intrinsic Josephson junctions of a cuprate superconductor with frequency-modulated terahertz radiation spectra
summary: Terahertz radiation from $\text{Bi}_2\text{Sr}_2\text{CaCu}_2\text{O}_{8+δ}$ intrinsic Josephson junctions (IJJs) provides an ideal platform to study the synchronization of a macroscopic quantum system. Here, we present a spectral analysis of a frequency-modulated Josephson plasma emitter coupled with patch antennas. In the unmodulated intensity distribution as a function of radiation frequency $I_{\mathrm{UM}}(ω)$, we observe a double Gaussian peak structure. Crucially, double-peak spectra obtained at a constant bias voltage imply either a rapid temporal distribution of resonances or their simultaneous excitation, driven by the mutual electromagnetic coupling between the IJJ mesa and the antennas. At low modulation frequencies $f_m$, the spectra are well reproduced by the products of $I_{\mathrm{UM}}(ω)$ and frequency combs, yielding a synchronized relaxation time $τ_s \simeq 0.28\text{ ns}$. Incorporating $τ_s$ quantitatively reproduces a drastic spectral transformation observed around $f_m \sim 1\text{ GHz}$, unveiling the sub-nanosecond non-equilibrium dynamics of coupled Josephson plasma.

title: Hallmark Signatures of Electronic Pairing in Two-Photon Two-Electron Coincidence Angle-Resolved Photoemission Spectroscopy
comment: 6 pages, 4 figures. Supplementary Material available upon request
summary: Understanding strongly correlated quantum materials remains a central challenge in condensed matter physics and materials science. While angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) has become an indispensable probe of single-quasiparticle excitations, it accesses electronic correlations only indirectly. Here we show that unlike one-photon in, two-electrons out coincidence ARPES ($γ\!\rightarrow\!2e$ 2eARPES), the two-photon in, two-electron out $2γ\!\rightarrow\!2e$ 2eARPES provides a direct and unambiguous probe of electronic pairing. We establish this on general theoretical grounds and substantiate it through large-scale numerical simulations of strongly correlated models with both paired and unpaired ground states. The key result is a model-independent separation in the $(ω_1,ω_2)$ plane of the two photoelectrons' energies, between signal from electrons emitted from the \emph{same} pair and signal from electrons emitted from \emph{different} pairs; this follows from energy conservation alone and is independent of any material-specific assumptions. Our findings demonstrate that $2γ\!\rightarrow\!2e$ 2eARPES can identify pairing and extract the pair binding energy as well as the energy of the 'glue' boson without any sophisticated data analysis or complementary measurements.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that two-photon in, two-electron out coincidence ARPES (2γ→2e 2eARPES) provides a direct and unambiguous probe of electronic pairing in strongly correlated quantum materials, unlike conventional one-photon ARPES which can only access correlations indirectly.
AI.motivation: Understanding strongly correlated quantum materials remains a central challenge, but existing ARPES techniques only indirectly access electronic correlations. There is a need for a direct probe of electronic pairing.
AI.method: The authors established the technique on general theoretical grounds and substantiated it through large-scale numerical simulations of strongly correlated models with both paired and unpaired ground states.
AI.result: The key finding is a model-independent separation in the (ω1, ω2) plane between signal from electrons emitted from the same pair versus different pairs, following from energy conservation alone and independent of material-specific assumptions. This allows identification of pairing and extraction of pair binding energy and 'glue' boson energy.
AI.conclusion: 2γ→2e 2eARPES can identify pairing and extract the pair binding energy as well as the energy of the 'glue' boson without any sophisticated data analysis or complementary measurements.

title: Role of Local Structural Variation in X-ray Photoelectron Spectrum of Silicon Oxide Interfaces
summary: We show that the broad X-ray photoelectron lines of silicon oxide on silicon arise from a continuous statistical distribution of core-level binding energies. Statistical simulations spanning compositions from Si to SiO$_2$ reproduce the full extent of this broadening, reaching 5 eV for SiO$_{1.0}$ , in quantitative agreement with 0.23 nm layer-resolved spectra reconstructed from Ar$^+$ sputtering data. This continuous distribution blurs distinct spectral fingerprints of local structural motifs, thereby challenging conventional chemical state assignment in oxide X-ray photoelectron spectra.

AI.tldr: The study reveals that broad X-ray photoelectron lines of silicon oxide on silicon originate from a continuous statistical distribution of core-level binding energies, rather than from distinct chemical states. Statistical simulations from Si to SiO2 successfully reproduced the observed broadening up to 5 eV for SiO1.0, matching 0.23 nm layer-resolved spectra from Ar+ sputtering data.
AI.motivation: Conventional XPS interpretation assumes that different chemical states produce distinct spectral peaks. However, silicon oxide exhibits unexpectedly broad lines that don't conform to this model, prompting investigation into the actual source of this broadening.
AI.method: Statistical simulations were conducted across compositions ranging from Si to SiO2, and results were validated against Ar+ sputtering data to reconstruct layer-resolved spectra at 0.23 nm resolution.
AI.result: The simulations reproduced the complete broadening extent (up to 5 eV for SiO1.0) in quantitative agreement with experimental layer-resolved spectra. The continuous binding energy distribution blurs distinct spectral fingerprints of local structural motifs.
AI.conclusion: This finding challenges conventional chemical state assignment in oxide XPS spectra, as the observed broad peaks result from continuous statistical distributions rather than discrete chemical states. This fundamentally questions standard interpretation methods for oxide materials.

title: Counterintuitive inverse superconducting transition beyond 4He-cooling limit
summary: Thermally driven quantum-orders observed in exceptional instances may redefine the role of thermal-fluctuation from a source of decoherence to a resource for coherent-state engineering. While preliminary signs of counterintuitive temperature-rise-triggered superconductivity manifested in CeCu2Si2, ErRh4B4, Ho1.2Mo6S8 and (La,Ce)Al2, their critical-temperatures (Tc-inv) remain below Kelvin-range, precluding substantial applications. Here, we report field-modulated inverse-superconducting-transitions above 4He-cooling-limit in Eu-based infinite-layer nickelates (EuxNd1-xNiO2 and EuxPr1-xNiO2) grown on a substrate under both overdoped and underdoped regimes. Paradigmatically, superconductivity with zero-resistance is confined between Tc-inv (2.6-5.4 K) and another higher normal-Tc, rising and decreasing with applied magnetic-field, respectively. Starting from the resistive-state below Tc-inv, the inverse-superconducting-transition is driven by not only temperature-rising, but also current-density, while superconductivity further vanishes at higher temperature and current thresholds. The Kelvin-range inverse superconducting transition is plausibly explained by temperature-induced alternating dominance of effective magnetic-fields arising from Eu2+4f7 related compensations relative to the upper-critical-field. Furthermore, an extended-phenomenological-framework is also supported by reemerged superconductivity below 300 mK under magnetic-field, giving rise to an unprecedented temperature-induced reentrant superconductivity. Our findings establish magnetic-interaction-reconfigured high-Tc systems as fertile platforms for exploring quantum phenomena that reverse thermal-decoherence paradigm, also enabling antithetical-designs to unlock untapped application-scenarios for quantum-phase-transition devices.

AI.tldr: This paper reports "inverse superconductivity" in Eu-based infinite-layer nickelates (EuₓNd₁₋ₓNiO₂ and EuₓPr₁₋ₓNiO₂) where zero-resistance superconductivity appears at higher temperatures upon heating, with reentrant superconductivity below 300 mK under magnetic field - challenging conventional thermal-decoherence paradigms.
AI.motivation: Conventional superconductivity is destroyed by thermal fluctuations. This work seeks to reverse that paradigm by finding systems where heating actually induces or enhances superconductivity, potentially enabling new quantum device applications.
AI.method: Eu-based infinite-layer nickelate thin films were grown under both overdoped and underdoped regimes. Electrical transport measurements were conducted while varying temperature, applied magnetic field, and current density to map out the phase diagram of inverse superconductivity.
AI.result: Zero-resistance superconductivity was confined between inverse Tc (2.6-5.4 K) and a higher normal Tc, with both boundaries modulated by applied magnetic field. Current density also drove the inverse superconducting transition. Reentrant superconductivity emerged below 300 mK under magnetic field.
AI.conclusion: Eu²⁺ 4f-related magnetic compensations create alternating effective magnetic field dominance that reverses thermal-decoherence, establishing magnetic-reconfigured high-Tc systems as novel platforms for quantum phase transition devices with anti-conventional design potential.

title: $d$-wave altermagnetism revealed by resonant inelastic X-ray scattering
summary: Altermagnetism defines a third fundamental class of collinear magnetic order, featuring compensated magnetic moments with antiparallel spin alignment, yet lifted Kramers degeneracy without the need for relativistic spin-orbit coupling. Its ability to host spin-polarized electronic bands and unconventional chiral magnons makes it a promising platform for functional materials. However, experimental verification has proven challenging; while circular dichroism in resonant inelastic X-ray scattering (RIXS) has been suggested as a signature of chiral magnons, it remains controversial whether this effect is an intrinsic property of altermagnetism or an artifact of experimental geometry. In this work, we resolve this debate and provide unambiguous experimental evidence of $d$-wave altermagnetism in the strongly correlated Lieb-lattice magnet La$_2$O$_3$Mn$_2$Se$_2$. The RIXS spectra exhibit a $d$-wave-symmetry circular dichroism in the magnetic excitations that vanishes in the paramagnetic phase. Through RIXS-operator symmetry analysis and exact-diagonalization calculations, we prove that the observed dichroism is a direct consequence of altermagnetic symmetry constraints, independent of magnon branch splitting. Our results provide definitive evidence for the experimental realization of $d$-wave altermagnetism in La$_2$O$_3$Mn$_2$Se$_2$ and establish circularly polarized RIXS as a highly symmetry-sensitive spectroscopic framework for detecting magnetic phases that evade conventional probes.

AI.tldr: This paper provides definitive experimental evidence for d-wave altermagnetism in the strongly correlated Lieb-lattice magnet La₂O₃Mn₂Se₂ using circular dichroism in resonant inelastic X-ray scattering (RIXS), resolving the controversy over whether this effect is intrinsic to altermagnetism or an experimental artifact.
AI.motivation: Altermagnetism represents a third fundamental class of collinear magnetic order, but experimental verification has been challenging. Circular dichroism in RIXS was suggested as a signature of chiral magnons, but remained controversial as to whether it was an intrinsic property or an artifact of experimental geometry.
AI.method: The authors used RIXS spectroscopy on La₂O₃Mn₂Se₂ combined with RIXS-operator symmetry analysis and exact-diagonalization calculations to investigate magnetic excitations and their symmetry properties across magnetic and paramagnetic phases.
AI.result: The RIXS spectra exhibit d-wave-symmetry circular dichroism in magnetic excitations that vanishes in the paramagnetic phase. Through combined experimental and theoretical analysis, the authors prove that this dichroism is a direct consequence of altermagnetic symmetry constraints, independent of magnon branch splitting.
AI.conclusion: This work provides definitive evidence for the experimental realization of d-wave altermagnetism in La₂O₃Mn₂Se₂ and establishes circularly polarized RIXS as a highly symmetry-sensitive spectroscopic framework for detecting magnetic phases that evade conventional probes.

title: Solution gate control of shallow silicon vacancy charge states in diamond
summary: Silicon-vacancy (SiV) centers in diamond combine near-infrared emission with solid-state robustness, but their performance hinges on isolating favorable defect charge states. We demonstrate static and dynamic control of ultra-shallow (<15 nm) SiV ensembles in type IIa diamond. By combining low-energy ion implantation with tailored oxygen and hydrogen terminations, we map regimes that maximise the fluorescent SiV- population over dark charge states. We then realize reversible SiV- to SiV0 conversion using aqueous electrolytic gating with sub-200 mV biases and low optical powers. Our results enable low-power electrical control of SiV ensembles for integrated quantum photonics and biologically compatible voltage imaging in the near-infrared.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates static and dynamic control of ultra-shallow (<15 nm) Silicon-vacancy (SiV) ensembles in type IIa diamond through low-energy ion implantation combined with oxygen and hydrogen terminations, achieving reversible SiV- to SiV0 conversion using aqueous electrolytic gating with sub-200 mV biases.
AI.motivation: Silicon-vacancy (SiV) centers in diamond offer near-infrared emission with solid-state robustness, but their performance is limited by the challenge of isolating favorable defect charge states; the goal is to enable low-power electrical control for integrated quantum photonics and biological voltage imaging.
AI.method: The researchers combined low-energy ion implantation with tailored oxygen and hydrogen terminations to map regimes that maximize the fluorescent SiV- population over dark charge states, then realized reversible SiV- to SiV0 conversion using aqueous electrolytic gating with sub-200 mV biases and low optical powers.
AI.result: They demonstrated static and dynamic control of ultra-shallow (<15 nm) SiV ensembles in type IIa diamond, successfully mapping regimes that maximize fluorescent SiV- population and achieving reversible SiV- to SiV0 conversion with minimal electrical and optical power requirements.
AI.conclusion: These results enable low-power electrical control of SiV ensembles for integrated quantum photonics and biologically compatible voltage imaging in the near-infrared, representing a significant advance for quantum sensing and bio-imaging applications.

title: The electric-field-driven intermediate state of three-dimensional superconductors
summary: The coexistence of superconductivity and finite electric fields may enable access to intriguing forms of electronic states. We demonstrate the emergence of an intermediate state in which electric fields penetrate the system while superconductivity still persists. Our measurements reveal a nonclassical regime characterized by the simultaneous presence of supercurrent and dissipative charge transport. This state, realized in a pristine unpatterned three-dimensional system, arises from electric-field-driven order parameter fluctuations. It provides a platform to explore dissipative states of charged quantum fluids far from equilibrium.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates an intermediate state where superconductivity and electric fields coexist in a 3D system, showing simultaneous supercurrent and dissipative charge transport arising from electric-field-driven order parameter fluctuations.
AI.motivation: The coexistence of superconductivity and finite electric fields may enable access to intriguing forms of electronic states, providing a platform to explore dissipative states of charged quantum fluids far from equilibrium.
AI.method: Measurements were conducted on a pristine unpatterned three-dimensional system to investigate the behavior under applied electric fields.
AI.result: The measurements revealed a nonclassical regime characterized by the simultaneous presence of supercurrent and dissipative charge transport, where electric fields penetrate the system while superconductivity still persists.
AI.conclusion: This intermediate state arises from electric-field-driven order parameter fluctuations and provides a new platform for exploring dissipative states of charged quantum fluids far from equilibrium.

title: Mimicry of chaos and $k$-design in higher order OTOCs of Luttinger liquids
summary: Out-of-time-order correlators (OTOCs) provide a fundamental metric for quantum chaos, but capturing the fine structure of information scrambling requires exploring their higher-order generalizations. Here, we systematically investigate the sequence of higher-order OTOCs in a Luttinger liquid and its lattice realization, the XXZ Heisenberg chain. Using bosonization and numerics, we extract the full temporal dynamics of the first three OTOCs, revealing that they rapidly erase memory of the initial state, and quickly saturate to their steady state values. Strikingly, we show that calculating the late time saturation values for the entire sequence of higher-order OTOCs maps exactly onto determining the partition function of a non-Hermitian Harper model. Through this mapping, we demonstrate that for moderately strong interactions, the steady-state OTOCs become parametrically small up to the seventh order, mimicking higher $k$-design. Our results reveal that Luttinger liquids exhibit an unexpectedly profound degree of apparent scrambling when viewed through the lens of higher-order OTOCs.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates higher-order out-of-time-order correlators (OTOCs) in Luttinger liquids and XXZ Heisenberg chains to understand information scrambling and quantum chaos. The study reveals that these systems rapidly erase memory of initial states and saturate to steady values. A key finding is that late-time OTOC values map to the partition function of a non-Hermitian Harper model, and for strong interactions, OTOCs up to 7th order become parametrically small, mimicking higher k-design behavior.
AI.motivation: Out-of-time-order correlators (OTOCs) are fundamental metrics for quantum chaos, but capturing the fine structure of information scrambling requires exploring higher-order generalizations. The study aims to systematically investigate this sequence of higher-order OTOCs in Luttinger liquids and their lattice realization.
AI.method: The researchers used bosonization techniques combined with numerical simulations to extract the full temporal dynamics of the first three higher-order OTOCs. They also established a mathematical mapping between late-time saturation values and the partition function of a non-Hermitian Harper model.
AI.result: The study found that higher-order OTOCs rapidly erase memory of the initial state and quickly saturate to steady-state values. For moderately strong interactions, the steady-state OTOCs become parametrically small up to the seventh order, mimicking higher k-design behavior. This reveals an unexpectedly profound degree of apparent scrambling in Luttinger liquids when viewed through higher-order OTOCs.
AI.conclusion: Luttinger liquids exhibit an unexpectedly profound degree of apparent scrambling when analyzed through higher-order OTOCs. The mapping to non-Hermitian Harper model provides a powerful analytical framework for understanding the late-time behavior of these correlators, suggesting that interactions can suppress higher-order OTOCs, creating behavior similar to higher k-designs.

title: First-Principles Study of Novel Lead-Free Double Perovskite \b{eta}2SnGeX6 (\b{eta} = K, Rb; X = Cl, Br, I) for thermomechanical, optoelectronic and outstanding thermoelectric applications
summary: In this study, the structural, mechanical, electronic, optical, and thermoelectric properties of the novel lead-free halide double perovskite series beta2SnGeX6 (beta = K, Rb; X = Cl, Br, I) are systematically investigated using density functional theory (DFT). Calculated formation energies, Tolerance factors, and octahedral factors confirm that all six compounds exhibit robust thermodynamic stability within a highly symmetric cubic geometry. Mechanical analysis derived from elastic parameters characterizes the entire series as fundamentally ductile, ensuring high processing elasticity and resistance to micro-cracking during device manufacturing. Electronic band structures reveal direct bandgaps showing exceptional composition-dependent tunability from 1.44 eV down to 0.64 eV via progressive halogen substitution. The wide gap chloride variations are optimized for single-junction photovoltaic absorbers, while the narrower-gap bromide and iodide analogs show immense promise for tandem solar architectures and near-infrared photodetectors. Thermoelectrically, heavy constituent atoms introduce strong lattice anharmonicity and intense high-temperature Umklapp phonon scattering, significantly suppressing lattice thermal conductivity. Combined with low carrier effective masses that optimize electrical transport, the iodide compounds achieve higher power factors and outstanding dimensionless figures of merit (ZT = 2.4 for K2SnGeI6 at 1000 K). Ultimately, these lead-free double perovskite family emerges as an environmentally benign and versatile platform for next-generation green optoelectronics and solid-state waste-heat recovery.

AI.tldr: A DFT study investigates structural, mechanical, electronic, optical, and thermoelectric properties of lead-free double perovskites β2SnGeX6 (β=K,Rb; X=Cl,Br,I). Compounds show tunable direct bandgaps (0.64-1.44 eV), ductile mechanical behavior, and exceptional thermoelectric performance (ZT=2.4 for K2SnGeI6 at 1000 K), offering promise for photovoltaics and waste-heat recovery.
AI.motivation: Develop environmentally benign, lead-free perovskite materials for next-generation green optoelectronics and solid-state thermoelectric waste-heat recovery applications.
AI.method: Density functional theory (DFT) calculations analyzing formation energies, tolerance factors, octahedral factors, elastic parameters, electronic band structures, and thermoelectric transport properties.
AI.result: All six compounds exhibit robust thermodynamic stability in cubic geometry. Direct bandgaps are tunable from 1.44 eV (Cl) to 0.64 eV (I). Chloride variants suit single-junction photovoltaics; bromide/iodide show potential for tandem solar cells and NIR photodetectors. Iodide compounds achieve high power factors with ZT=2.4 for K2SnGeI6 at 1000 K.
AI.conclusion: The lead-free β2SnGeX6 double perovskite family emerges as a versatile, environmentally friendly platform for next-generation green optoelectronics and thermoelectric energy conversion.

title: Room-Temperature Calcium Intercalation into Graphite Catalyzed by Sodium
summary: Calcium (Ca) insertion into graphite (C) has been considered to require elevated temperatures, and its occurrence at room temperature (RT) has been regarded as highly unlikely. Here, we demonstrate that sodium (Na) catalysis enables the formation of superconducting CaC$_6$ even at RT. In mixtures of Ca, Na, and graphite, the gradual development of superconducting diamagnetism and the emergence of X-ray diffraction peaks confirm the formation of CaC$_6$ during storage at RT. The superconducting transition temperature increases with storage time, and the amount of CaC$_6$ scales proportionally to the square root of storage time. These findings provide new insights into the mechanism of superconductivity and Na-catalyzed formation of CaC$_6$, and highlight the potential of this RT intercalation process for practical applications such as electrode materials in Ca-ion batteries.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that sodium (Na) catalysis enables the formation of superconducting CaC6 at room temperature (RT), which was previously thought to require elevated temperatures. Through mixtures of Ca, Na, and graphite, the authors observed the gradual development of superconducting diamagnetism and X-ray diffraction peaks confirming CaC6 formation during storage at RT.
AI.motivation: Calcium insertion into graphite has been considered to require elevated temperatures, and its occurrence at room temperature has been regarded as highly unlikely. This research seeks to explore whether room temperature intercalation is possible through catalysis.
AI.method: The researchers prepared mixtures of calcium (Ca), sodium (Na), and graphite, then stored them at room temperature. They monitored the formation of CaC6 through measurements of superconducting diamagnetism and X-ray diffraction over time.
AI.result: The gradual development of superconducting diamagnetism and the emergence of X-ray diffraction peaks confirmed the formation of CaC6 during storage at RT. The superconducting transition temperature increased with storage time, and the amount of CaC6 formed scaled proportionally to the square root of storage time.
AI.conclusion: These findings provide new insights into the mechanism of superconductivity and Na-catalyzed formation of CaC6, and highlight the potential of this RT intercalation process for practical applications such as electrode materials in Ca-ion batteries.

title: Emergence of Resonating Valence-Bond Correlations in Stretched Graphene
summary: Electronic correlations in graphene are generally considered weak due to the large bandwidth of its $π$ electrons. Here we show that tensile expansion of the honeycomb lattice provides a direct route to enhancing correlation effects. Using variational and diffusion quantum Monte Carlo, we compare a conventional Jastrow-Slater determinant wave function with a resonating-valence-bond (RVB) Jastrow-antisymmetrized geminal product ansatz for a series of stretched graphene lattices. We find that the energy gain of the RVB state relative to the single-determinant description increases with bond expansion up to a critical strain $δ_{\mathrm{cr}}$, and decreases beyond it, revealing a nonmonotonic evolution of electronic correlations. The crossover is found to occur in the range $15\% < δ_{\mathrm{cr}} < 20\%$, in agreement with mechanical stability limits. This behavior indicates a transition from a weakly correlated Dirac semimetal to a regime with enhanced non-dynamic correlation and short-range singlet pairing. Our results provide direct many-body evidence that lattice expansion drives graphene into a regime where RVB-like correlations become energetically favorable, offering a simple route to tuning correlation effects in Dirac materials.

AI.tldr: Tensile strain in graphene enhances electronic correlations nonmonotonically, with a critical strain of 15-20% where RVB-like correlations become favorable, marking a transition from weakly correlated Dirac semimetal to enhanced correlation regime.
AI.motivation: Electronic correlations in graphene are typically considered weak due to large π-electron bandwidth. This study aims to explore whether lattice expansion can directly enhance correlation effects, which could provide a simple tuning mechanism for Dirac materials.
AI.method: Variational quantum Monte Carlo (VQMC) and diffusion quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) methods were used to compare two wave function ansatz: conventional Jastrow-Slater determinant and resonating-valence-bond (RVB) Jastrow-antisymmetrized geminal product, across various strain levels in graphene.
AI.result: The RVB state's energy gain relative to single-determinant increases with bond expansion up to critical strain δ_cr (15% < δ_cr < 20%), then decreases beyond it. This reveals nonmonotonic evolution of electronic correlations, with crossover occurring within mechanical stability limits.
AI.conclusion: Lattice expansion drives graphene into a regime where RVB-like correlations become energetically favorable, transitioning from weakly correlated Dirac semimetal to enhanced non-dynamic correlation with short-range singlet pairing. This provides a simple route to tuning correlation effects in Dirac materials.

title: Self-Aligned Metallic-Semiconducting Phosphorus Nanoarrays Driven by Facet Engineering
summary: Two-dimensional (2D) materials often require specific substrate terminations for epitaxial stabilization, yet the search for suitable templates has largely focused on low-index metal surfaces, which may not provide the optimal conditions for the growth of new phases. Here, we show that crystal-facet engineering on curved Cu surfaces enables the stabilization, within a single preparation step, of two distinct 2D phosphorus phases with different electronic properties. Hexagonal blue phosphorene forms on Cu(111) terraces, whereas a previously unreported skewed-square phosphorus phase is stabilized on Cu(513) facets. By combining complementary microscopy and spectroscopy techniques with theoretical calculations, we determine the structural and electronic properties of this new phase, which displays semiconducting character, in contrast to the metallic behavior of blue phosphorene. The coexistence of these two competing phases gives rise to a metal-to-semiconducting transition of the 2D phosphorus layer over the substrate. Locally, the competition between the two phases gives rise to self-aligned nanoarrays of alternating metallic and semiconducting phosphorus terraces. These results establish crystal-facet engineering as a practical route for discovering and stabilizing emergent 2D material phases on high-index substrates, while also enabling the engineering of nanostructures with tailored electronic properties through a simple and scalable growth process.

AI.tldr: Crystal-facet engineering on curved Cu surfaces enables simultaneous stabilization of two distinct 2D phosphorus phases (hexagonal blue phosphorene on Cu(111) and a new skewed-square phase on Cu(513)), enabling a metal-to-semiconducting transition and self-aligned nanoarrays with tunable electronic properties.
AI.motivation: Two-dimensional materials often require specific substrate terminations for epitaxial stabilization, yet the search for suitable templates has largely focused on low-index metal surfaces, which may not provide the optimal conditions for the growth of new phases.
AI.method: Using crystal-facet engineering on curved Cu surfaces and combining complementary microscopy and spectroscopy techniques with theoretical calculations.
AI.result: Two distinct 2D phosphorus phases were stabilized: hexagonal blue phosphorene forms on Cu(111) terraces, while a previously unreported skewed-square phosphorus phase forms on Cu(513) facets. The new phase displays semiconducting character, in contrast to the metallic behavior of blue phosphorene. The coexistence creates a metal-to-semiconducting transition and self-aligned nanoarrays of alternating metallic and semiconducting phosphorus terraces.
AI.conclusion: Crystal-facet engineering on high-index substrates is a practical route for discovering and stabilizing emergent 2D material phases, while also enabling engineering of nanostructures with tailored electronic properties through a simple and scalable growth process.

title: Controlling magnetic domain walls with supercurrents
summary: Establishing a versatile, fast and reliable magnetic memory technology is a giant bottleneck for cryogenic computing since present-day room-temperature solutions either cease to work or consume too much power. The long-term goal of superconducting spintronics has been to overcome this bottleneck by generating magnetic memories with equal-spin triplet supercurrent driven through them to control their magnetization direction. This path has been hampered by the short spin relaxation length and strong anisotropy in ferromagnets. Here we show how the supercurrent driven generation of spin accumulation in a superconductor/magnetic insulator bilayer, together with Gilbert damping of magnetization lead to a motion of magnetic domain walls. This manifests as a local voltage across the wall, which allows its position to be identified. Associated with this voltage and the current, there is Joule power which is dissipated via the Gilbert damping. The power required to maintain domain wall motion is orders of magnitude smaller than in the normal state, where most of the power is wasted in producing the current.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a method for generating magnetic domain wall motion in superconductor/magnetic insulator bilayers using supercurrent-driven spin accumulation and Gilbert damping, enabling low-power cryogenic memory technology.
AI.motivation: The main bottleneck for cryogenic computing is the lack of versatile, fast, and reliable magnetic memory technology. Existing room-temperature solutions either fail at cryogenic temperatures or consume excessive power. The long-term goal of superconducting spintronics has been to overcome this by using equal-spin triplet supercurrent to control magnetization direction, but this approach has been limited by short spin relaxation length and strong anisotropy in ferromagnets.
AI.method: The researchers demonstrate that supercurrent-driven spin accumulation in a superconductor/magnetic insulator bilayer, combined with Gilbert damping of magnetization, can induce magnetic domain wall motion. This motion produces a local voltage across the wall, allowing its position to be identified. The Joule power associated with this voltage and current is dissipated through Gilbert damping.
AI.result: The power required to maintain domain wall motion is orders of magnitude smaller than in the normal state, where most of the power is wasted in producing the current.
AI.conclusion: This approach offers a promising solution for creating low-power cryogenic magnetic memory devices, potentially overcoming the fundamental limitations of current technologies in cryogenic computing environments.

title: Mapping the non-equilibrium interacting Anderson Impurity Model to an effective Gaussian theory
summary: Quantum impurity models with strong electron correlations, such as the paradigmatic Anderson Impurity Model (AIM), are central to our understanding of a range of physical phenomena including local moment formation, Coulomb blockade and Kondo screening. They describe magnetic atoms and molecules on surfaces, quantum dot circuits, and correlated materials through dynamical mean field theory. The physics of such systems in strongly non-equilibrium conditions is particularly complex and challenging to capture, whereas Gaussian models of free fermions can be easily solved. Here we show that the time-evolving dynamics of the AIM after a quench can be described by a completely non-interacting version of the model, at the expense of coupling to additional static auxiliary degrees of freedom. Starting from the full solution of the quenched AIM using ED and DMRG, we study the properties of this mapping using numerical optimization, and uncover intriguing structure in the auxiliary system. The method allows us to understand interacting non-equilibrium dynamics through the simpler lens of an effective non-interacting system of larger dimension.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that the complex dynamics of the Anderson Impurity Model (AIM) after a quantum quench can be mapped onto a non-interacting system coupled to auxiliary degrees of freedom, enabling simpler description of strongly correlated non-equilibrium physics.
AI.motivation: Quantum impurity models like AIM are crucial for understanding various physical phenomena including local moment formation, Coulomb blockade, and Kondo screening. However, their non-equilibrium dynamics are extremely complex and challenging to capture theoretically. The motivation is to find a simpler theoretical framework to describe these strongly interacting systems.
AI.method: The authors use exact diagonalization (ED) and density matrix renormalization group (DMRG) methods to solve the quenched AIM, then employ numerical optimization to study the mapping to an effective non-interacting system with auxiliary degrees of freedom.
AI.result: The time-evolving dynamics of AIM after a quench can be described by a completely non-interacting version of the model at the cost of coupling to additional static auxiliary degrees of freedom. The mapping reveals intriguing structure in the auxiliary system.
AI.conclusion: This method provides a way to understand interacting non-equilibrium dynamics through the simpler lens of an effective non-interacting system of larger dimension, potentially simplifying the theoretical treatment of strongly correlated quantum systems out of equilibrium.

title: Spin-orbit coupling renormalization of the natural optical activity of Pb5Ge3O11 from first-principles
summary: We present a first-principles study of the natural optical activity of the gyroelectric Pb$_5$Ge$_3$O$_{11}$ crystal, explicitly accounting for spin-orbit coupling (SOC) effects. We derive a new analytical expression for the gyration coefficients within the recent framework of long-wavelength density-functional perturbation theory [Phys. Rev. Lett. \textbf{131}, 086902 (2023)], which significantly improves computational efficiency by reducing the number of required response functions and includes spin-orbit coupling effects. We use this implementation to investigate the evolution of Pb$_5$Ge$_3$O$_{11}$'s optical rotation across the ferroelectric double-well, from the paraelectric $P\bar{6}$ phase to the ferroelectric $P3$ phase. Our results demonstrate that, in addition to the substantial renormalization of the double-well energy, spin-orbit coupling contributions play an equally crucial role in the natural optical activity, largely through purely electronic contributions, while SOC-induced structural relaxation effects are minor.

AI.tldr: Summary generation failed
AI.motivation: Motivation analysis unavailable
